title: The North Declares Itself
text: Three garrisons fold in a fortnight. The separatist front declares an independent Azawad from Kidal; the militants who rode in alongside them are already renaming the courts. The capital's army blames the politicians, loudly, on the radio.
notes: The 2012 opening beat. The army-blames-politicians line is the coup cascade's first seed, planted in text.
sources: 2012 Azawad declaration record

title: The Window
text: The columns are moving south; the airport at Sévaré is hours from falling. Bamako is formally asking. Every ally is watching to see whether you answer, and every future recruiter is watching how.
notes: The Serval moment. Intervention is genuinely good now — the welcome is real (Kilcullen); what you do with the welcome is the next decade.
sources: 2013 intervention record

title: The Franchise Arrives
text: A splinter katiba posts its pledge to the global brand. The men are the same men; the flag and the ambition are new. The corridor between Ménaka and the tri-border begins to change hands at night.

choices.label: Note the split — rivals fight each other

notes: The 2015 ISGS split, composited. Both choices seed the franchise — history's gravity — but the shape differs.
sources: ISGS emergence record

title: The Square Fills in Ouagadougou
text: A quarter-century presidency tries to amend its own term limits, and the capital answers in the streets. The palace falls in two days; a transition of soldiers and ministers inherits an administration mid-crisis. None of this is about your war — and all of it will be.

choices.label: Keep working with whoever holds the file

notes: The October 2014 insurrection and Compaoré's fall — the missing beat that hollowed the Burkinabè state before the spillover arrived. Political texture the system cannot generate (§18.9); the spillover itself stays endogenous.
sources: Burkina Faso 2014 insurrection record

title: The Street and the Barracks
text: A contested election, a disputed court, a summer of crowds. The army watches the crowds, and the officers you trained watch the army. Your ambassador's cables have stopped using the word 'stable'.
notes: The 2020 pre-coup texture. The coup itself stays endogenous (collapse roll) — this beat only loads the dice the way history loaded them.
sources: Mali 2020 crisis record

title: The Regional Bloc Draws a Line
text: The regional community announces borders closed and accounts frozen until a transition timetable appears. The junta answers with flags and a rally. Prices rise in the markets first; speeches about sovereignty rise second.
notes: Sanctions pressure with the documented humanitarian-blowback channel: the grievance lands on the population before the junta (§7 diplomatic).
sources: ECOWAS-Mali sanctions record 2022

title: Advisors Without Insignia
text: Charter flights at night; weights manifested as 'mining equipment'. Within a month the junta's communiqués use new vocabulary, and a concession map you've never seen is circulating in the ministries. Their offer came with no conditions at all.
notes: The mercenary patron lands either way — the market mechanic's truth (§8): when a junta forms, the no-strings offer wins by default. You only choose what it costs you.
sources: Wagner/Africa Corps Mali deployment record

title: An Alliance of the Like-Minded
text: Three flags on one stage. A mutual-defense pact, a promised joint force, talk of a common currency — and a communiqué naming you, specifically, as the region's problem. The pooled broadcast reach starts that evening.
notes: The AES beat. The consolidation clock itself lands v0.7 — at v0.4 the proto-bloc detection plus this card carry the weight in text and price.
sources: Alliance of Sahel States formation record 2023

title: The Concord of Enemies
text: Two factions that spent a decade killing each other hit five positions in one night, in sequence, on a shared clock. The communiqués are separate; the staff work plainly wasn't. Your analysts called this 'unlikely' in last quarter's assessment.
notes: The April 2026 model: ideologically unlike groups, joint offensive (§5.2). Requires an actual link in the faction graph — if the player kept them apart, this card never exists. The strength surge lands either way; the response shapes whose ledger pays.
sources: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Mali_offensives

title: Partner-Force Atrocity
text: A unit you raid alongside is credibly accused of executing detainees in a village sweep. Your flag is on the operation. The report is on your desk; the press does not have it yet.

choices.label: Disclose and open a joint inquiry

notes: The Transparency Dial (§6). Disclose: pay now, less Local damage (honesty registers). Bury: nothing now, but a leak clock starts — and a free press tends to find out, at a multiple of the honest cost.
sources: Sahel partner-force abuse reporting
DESIGN §6 Transparency Dial

title: The Strike Hit a Wedding
text: Your strike cell is certain the convoy was a target. The village is certain it was a wedding. Both cannot be right, and only one of them will hold a press conference. Your name is on the authorization.

choices.label: Own it: release the assessment, pay compensation

notes: The Transparency Dial again, harder: classifying costs nothing today and risks an election-shock leak later. The disclose branch is cheaper on average — if you can stomach paying every time.
sources: Drone-strike collateral reporting; DESIGN §6 Transparency Dial

title: Refugee Wave
text: Displacement from the contested sectors crosses a threshold; columns are moving toward the capital and the borders. Cameras are already there.

choices.label: Press the host state to close the corridor

notes: Generic deck, shared with Sahel-lite.
sources: UNHCR Sahel displacement record

title: Base Access Revoked
text: The junta's spokesman, flanked by new advisors you recognize from elsewhere, announces that your forces have weeks to leave. The advisors' employer does not do press conferences.

choices.label: Comply and withdraw in good order
Pay to stay — drop the conditionality

notes: Generic deck, shared with Sahel-lite.
sources: 2022 French withdrawal from Mali record

title: Election Shock at Home
text: A coffin-count graphic leads the evening news for the third night running. The opposition has found its single issue, and it is your war.

choices.label: Defend the mission on its merits

notes: Generic deck, shared with Sahel-lite.
sources: Merom, How Democracies Lose Small Wars

title: A 'Transitional Council' Announces Itself
text: Officers from a battalion your trainers certified appear on state television at dawn. They speak of corruption, of the failing war, and of the patriotic necessity of a transitional period. They thank their foreign partners — ambiguously.
notes: Generic deck, shared with Sahel-lite.
sources: Sahel coup-leader training records

title: The Quiet Sector
text: Incident counts in a sector you barely cover have fallen for two consecutive quarters. Your briefer calls it progress. A footnote mentions new 'dispute-resolution committees' operating in the villages — author unknown.
notes: Generic deck, shared with Sahel-lite.
sources: 2026 Sahel entrenchment analyses — falling attacks ≠ security

title: The Cordon
text: For five days nothing leaves the market town. When a fact-finding mission finally counts, the dead are in the hundreds — and they were executed, not killed in any battle. The junta's bulletin reads 'jihadists neutralised.' Its foreign partner does not give interviews.

choices.label: Fund the documentation — satellites, forensics, the survivors' testimony
Note it. The war will not wait for a tribunal

notes: Closed-society layer (§20.2): mass atrocity rendered as aftermath and number, not act. Document vs. look away.
sources: UN OHCHR fact-finding mission: Moura, Mali, 27–31 March 2022 — at least 500 unlawfully killed by FAMa and foreign (Wagner) personnel
Human Rights Watch, on Moura and the 2023 Russian veto

title: What Everyone Knows
text: Staged footage multiplies across a thousand day-old accounts: bodies at a base your forces just vacated, captioned in your name. A Moscow 'news agency' assembled from a mercenary group's old contacts runs it first. By morning the lie is 'what everyone knows.'

choices.label: Fund attribution research — trace the network, publish the forensics

notes: §20.3 the lying layer: the contested information space. Pairs with the atrocity card.
sources: EEAS–VIGINUM–FCDO technical report on the 'African Initiative' Russian FIMI operation, June 2025
Staged-corpse disinformation at Gossi, Mali, April 2022, falsely blaming departing French forces

title: Until Further Notice
text: A single decree suspends every political party 'until further notice,' and the transition's calendar quietly loses its last date. The men who signed it will not be standing for election against anyone.

choices.label: Back exile media and designate the officials who signed it
Work with the new reality — you still need the basing rights

notes: §20.2 repression by decree: party dissolution, indefinite rule.
sources: Mali suspends then dissolves political parties, April 2024; Burkina Faso dissolves all parties by decree, January 2026 (HRW; Al Jazeera)

title: Mobilised
text: A human-rights lawyer who had been cataloguing the army's abuses is filmed in fresh fatigues at the front — 'mobilised' by emergency decree. Courts rule the orders manifestly illegal; the orders keep arriving. Every other critic reads the message.

choices.label: Shelter the conscripted voices and carry their record out
Stay clear of their internal affairs

notes: §20.2 punitive conscription of critics.
sources: Burkina Faso's April 2023 'general mobilization' decree used to forcibly conscript journalists, judges and activists; courts ruled it illegal (HRW; Amnesty International — the Daouda Diallo case)

title: The Quiet
text: Another rung down the ladder: the foreign broadcasters go off the air, then the wire services, then the apps. A new statute makes sharing 'data likely to disturb public order' a crime. The region goes quiet — which is not the same thing as calm.

choices.label: Fund exile and diaspora outlets to route around the blackout

notes: §20.2 the media-ban ladder — quiet is not calm (the §9 deceptive-calm thesis, socialised).
sources: Sahel media-ban ladder: France 24, RFI and the BBC suspended and banned across Mali, Burkina Faso and Niger, 2023–2024 (CPJ; RSF)

title: Who Is Left to Count
text: Your resolution to renew the independent panel — the last body still naming names — dies on a single veto. The peacekeepers are already gone. From here, what is recorded depends entirely on who is left to record it.

choices.label: Build the evidence outside the UN — fund the independent record
Accept the limits of the instrument

notes: §20.3/§20.4: the rival's veto nullifies the UN instrument — a deliberately frustrating tool.
sources: MINUSMA terminated at the junta's demand, UN Security Council Res. 2690, June 2023
Russia's lone veto ending the Mali sanctions Panel of Experts mandate, August 2023 (HRW)

title: Struck Off
text: The designations you spent so much capital to win are quietly lifted — three names removed to 'court security cooperation' with the very officers they named. Impunity, it turns out, is also a policy. What endures is not the punishment you sought; it is the record you funded.

choices.label: Keep funding the record; documentation outlives the sanction
Spend your outrage where it can still move something

notes: §20.4 the sobering counterweight: documentation exposes; it rarely punishes. The durable win is the record.
sources: US lifts sanctions on three Wagner-linked Malian officials to court security cooperation, March 2026 (HRW); Wagner rebranded as the state-run Africa Corps
